title: Cook
desc: Tested recipes. Filter by cuisine, course, diet, time, and difficulty.

title: Explore
desc: Three families gather every entry. Pick a family for a focused walk, or jump straight to a category.

title: Pantry
desc: What ingredients are, how to choose and store them, what to swap. Plus the equipment that earns its drawer.

title: Skills
desc: Methods that show up across many recipes. Cuisines built from a pantry. Curated reading paths.

title: Weeknight pasta
desc: Pasta dishes that hit the table in under 30 minutes without sacrificing technique.

title: All-purpose flour
desc: Wheat flour at 10–12% protein. The middle of the road — strong enough for bread in a pinch, soft enough for cake if you handle it gently.

title: Assam tea
desc: Robust, malty black tea from northeast India. The default base for masala chai and what gives English Breakfast its backbone.

title: Black peppercorns
desc: Dried unripe fruit of Piper nigrum. Heat from piperine; aroma from terpenes that vanish on grinding.

title: Butter (unsalted)
desc: Cow's-milk fat with ~16% water and ~2% milk solids. The solids are the flavor lever in cooking.

title: Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋)
desc: Aged glutinous-rice vinegar from Zhenjiang. Dark, malty, smoky, and only mildly acidic — closer in spirit to balsamic than to white vinegar.

title: Cinnamon stick
desc: Dried inner bark of Cinnamomum trees. Two main types: hot, sharp Cassia (the supermarket default) and floral, gentler Ceylon ("true" cinnamon).

title: Dark soy sauce (老抽)
desc: Aged longer than light soy and cut with molasses or caramel. Used in small amounts for color and depth, not for primary salting.

title: Eggs (large)
desc: Roughly 50g per large egg: ~30g white, ~18g yolk, ~6g shell. The white sets at 62°C, the yolk at 70°C — that gap is half of what you cook with.

title: Garlic
desc: Allium sativum. Pungency comes from allicin, which only forms when you cut or crush a clove — and which mellows quickly with heat.

title: Ginger (fresh)
desc: Rhizome of Zingiber officinale. Sharp, peppery heat from gingerol when raw; mellower and sweeter (zingerone) when cooked.

title: Light soy sauce (生抽)
desc: The everyday Chinese seasoning soy — saltier, thinner, and lighter in color than dark soy. This is what most stir-fries and dipping sauces actually want.

title: Pecorino romano
desc: Hard sheep's-milk cheese from Lazio. Salty, sharp, lactic — the canonical cheese for Roman pasta.

title: Chinese sesame paste (芝麻酱)
desc: Toasted sesame seeds ground to a paste. Darker, deeper, and more bitter than tahini — the toast is the whole point.

title: Sichuan peppercorns
desc: The husks of Zanthoxylum berries. Not actually pepper — they create má, the buzzing tingling numbness that defines Sichuan cooking.

title: Star anise
desc: The dried fruit of Illicium verum, eight-pointed and intensely licorice-aromatic. The soul of Chinese five-spice and the unmistakable note in Thai iced tea.
